name: iSkin: Flexible, Stretchable and Visually Customizable On-Body Touch Sensors
short_description: A thin, flexible, stretchable on-skin sensor overlay that turns the body's surface into a touch input area for mobile and wearable computing.
long_description: iSkin is a class of skin-worn touch sensors built from biocompatible silicone with embedded conductive carbon-black electrodes, combining capacitive and resistive sensing to detect touch at two pressure levels even when the overlay is stretched by 30% or bent to a 0.5 cm radius. Produced in customizable shapes for the finger, forearm, or ear, it supports buttons, sliders, and click wheels worn directly on the skin, enabling discreet, always-available input. Presented at ACM CHI 2015 (Best Paper Award), it matters to physical AI and interaction design as an early on-body input surface that explores how computation can become continuous with the human body rather than confined to a rigid device.
ai_note: iSkin is a passive capacitive/resistive touch-sensing material; touch detection is signal thresholding with no AI or machine learning involved.

images.caption: iSkin stretchable on-body touch sensor overlay worn on the skin as a customizable input surface.
images.alt_text: A thin, semi-transparent silicone sensor sticker with printed touch buttons applied directly to a person's skin, used as a touch input surface.

sources.title: iSkin: Flexible, Stretchable and Visually Customizable On-Body Touch Sensors for Mobile Computing
